coredata.dc:title: Oppositely sensitive pH-Responsive surfactants derived from polymer-grafted carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tianhao
Fan, Junjie
Qiu, Wenxiu
Yuan, Xinyu
Hu, Jianwen
Gao, Haotian
Xie, Wenqing
Li, Chunling
Sun, Shuangqing
Hu, Songqing
coredata.dc:description: This study reports the development of two pH-responsive Pickering emulsifiers utilizing carbon dots (CDs) as the core component. Both emulsifiers were fabricated via a post-synthetic method, involving the grafting of poly [2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate] (PDMAEMA) onto the surface of CDs to create CD-based emulsifier. Notably, despite employing an identical synthetic route, the two CD-based emulsifiers exhibit markedly distinct pH-responsive behaviors. The findings demonstrate that both CD-based emulsifiers effectively stabilize Pickering emulsions, and their emulsification and demulsification behaviors can be precisely controlled by adjusting the pH, displaying excellent reproducibility in their pH-responsive performance. To elucidate the underlying reason for this differing pH-responsiveness, the concept of hydrophilic-lipophilic balance (HLB) was introduced. The analysis of the HLB values of the two CD-based emulsifiers revealed that their hydrophilic-lipophilic properties exhibit opposite trends under differing pH conditions, contributing to their contrasting pH-responsive behaviors. This study not only lays a fundamental basis foundation and offers guidance for further research and applications of CD-based smart-responsive emulsifiers but also presents novel insights into design and development of more intelligent and functional emulsifying agents. Moreover, owing to the enhanced fluorescence characteristics of CDs, these pH-responsive emulsifiers hold significant potential for interdisciplinary applications, such as oil and gas exploration and fluorescent labelling.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Emulsification and demulsification of pickering emulsions
Synthesis and characterization of CD NPs
Fabrication and characterization of CD-3-stabilized pickering emulsion
Effect of oil-to-water ratio on emulsion stability
Effect of nanoparticle concentration on emulsion stability
Effect of salt ion concentration on emulsion stability
pH-responsive behavior of CD-3-stabilized pickering emulsion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication process of CD-3 through post-synthetic modification. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a, b) TEM images of M-CD-1 and H-CD-1, respectively. (c, d) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of M-CD-1 and H-CD-1, respectively. (e) XRD patterns of both carbon dots. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a, b) FTIR spectra of M-CD and H-CD, (c) 1H NMR spectra of M-CD-3 and H-CD-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XPS Spectra of Various Carbon Dots. (a–c) XPS spectra of H-CD-1, including (a) the scanning spectrum of C1s, (b) the scanning spectrum of N1s, and (c) the scanning spectrum of O1s. (d–f) XPS spectra of H-CD-3, showing (d) the scanning spectrum of C1s, (e) the scanning spectrum of N1s, and (f) the scanning spectrum of O1s. (g–i) XPS spectra of M-CD-1, comprising (g) the scanning spectrum of C1s, (h) the scanning spectrum of N1s, and (i) the scanning spectrum of O1s. (j–l) XPS spectra of M-CD-3, displaying (j) the scanning spectrum of C1s, (k) the scanning spectrum of N1s, and (l) the scanning spectrum of O1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Images of the miniature model under different experimental conditions: (a) Initial conditions, (b) In brine, (c) In brine with 500 ppm H-CD-3, and (d) In brine with M-CD-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Optical and microscopic images of Pickering emulsions stabilized by H-CD-3 (a) and M-CD-3 (b) with varying paraffin oil to water ratios. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Optical and microscopic images of Pickering emulsions with varying concentration of H-CD-3 (top row) and M-CD-3 (bottom row). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Microscopic images and particle size distribution of emulsion droplets (H-CD-3 at monovalent salt ions concentration of 1 mol/L (a), 2 mol/L (b), 3 mol/L (c), 4 mol/L (d), respectively. M-CD-3 at monovalent salt ions concentration of 1 mol/L (f), 2 mol/L (g), 3 mol/L (h), 4 mol/L (i), respectively.). And partitioning of H-CD-3(e), M-CD-3(j) at different monovalent ion concentrations. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic diagram of Pickering emulsion stabilized by pH-responsive CD-3, (b) Specific behaviors of the two emulsifiers under different pH conditions,(c) Emulsion state after seven cycles of H-CD-3 response and after five cycles of M-CD-3 response. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Trends in pH and Zeta potential during the response of H-CD-3 (a) and M-CD-3 (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Variation of water separation over time for two emulsifiers emulsifying oils with different HLB values. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Optical photographs and micrographs of CD-3 emulsified oils with varying HLB values.

head.ce:title: Oppositely sensitive pH-Responsive surfactants derived from polymer-grafted carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports the development of two pH-responsive Pickering emulsifiers utilizing carbon dots (CDs) as the core component. Both emulsifiers were fabricated via a post-synthetic method, involving the grafting of poly [2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate] (PDMAEMA) onto the surface of CDs to create CD-based emulsifier. Notably, despite employing an identical synthetic route, the two CD-based emulsifiers exhibit markedly distinct pH-responsive behaviors. The findings demonstrate that both CD-based emulsifiers effectively stabilize Pickering emulsions, and their emulsification and demulsification behaviors can be precisely controlled by adjusting the pH, displaying excellent reproducibility in their pH-responsive performance. To elucidate the underlying reason for this differing pH-responsiveness, the concept of hydrophilic-lipophilic balance (HLB) was introduced. The analysis of the HLB values of the two CD-based emulsifiers revealed that their hydrophilic-lipophilic properties exhibit opposite trends under differing pH conditions, contributing to their contrasting pH-responsive behaviors. This study not only lays a fundamental basis foundation and offers guidance for further research and applications of CD-based smart-responsive emulsifiers but also presents novel insights into design and development of more intelligent and functional emulsifying agents. Moreover, owing to the enhanced fluorescence characteristics of CDs, these pH-responsive emulsifiers hold significant potential for interdisciplinary applications, such as oil and gas exploration and fluorescent labelling.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials have gradually emerged as a research hotspot in recent years, owing to their noteworthy advantages such as environmental friendliness stability, and versatility. Among these, carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging class of zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterial, have rapidly garnered the attention of researchers since their discovery. Their excellent optical properties [1,2], low toxicity [3], eco-friendliness [4], excellent biocompatibility [5], and facile preparation [6] have shown immense potential across diverse fields such as biomedicine [7], anti-counterfeiting [8], sensing [9,10], catalysis [11], light-emitting diodes [12], and photovoltaic devices [13]. The unique properties of CDs stem mainly from their nanoscale dimensions, typically ranging from 1 to 10 nm, along with their distinctive structures. Various synthesis methods have been devised to produce these dots, including solvothermal [14], microwave [15], template [16] and combustion [17] techniques. Notably, the surfaces of these synthesized carbon dots are adorned with abundant functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups, offering crucial chemical sites for subsequent polymer grafting.

Emulsions occupy a pivotal position in industries spanning agriculture, food processing, medicine, petrochemicals and beyond. However, environmental concerns associated with traditional surfactants and their non-recyclability have posed significant challenges. Pickering emulsions have addressed these issues by substituting surfactants with solid particles [18], resulting in enhanced stability, reduced solid particle dosage and excellent mechanical properties, thereby broadening their application horizons across various fields [19]. Nevertheless, in certain application, emulsions necessitate destabilization through additional physical methods (e.g., centrifugation or shear) or chemical breakers, thereby increasing economic and environmental costs [20]. Stimulus-responsive Pickering emulsions effectively solve this issue by simply changing external environments, such as pH [21], temperature [22], salt concentration [23], and magnetic field [24], enabling a controlled shift between emulsion stability and instability. Among these, pH responsiveness has garnered considerable attention due to its ease of implementation and broad applications [25]. pH-responsive solid particles can be broadly categorized into three primary groups: those modified with amphiphilic molecules or polymers [26,27]; those synthesized as microgels or inorganic composites incorporating pH-sensitive polymers [28–30]; and those utilizing electrostatic attraction for the modification of hydrophilic particles [31,32].

DMAEMA graft copolymers have demonstrated substantial potential for applications in pH-responsive materials [33] and macromolecular synthesis [34]. DMAEMA exhibits remarkable pH responsiveness due to its weakly basic tertiary amino groups, which undergo protonation and deprotonation transitions, leading to the expansion and contraction of the polymer chain [35]. This hydrophilic-hydrophobic transition enables to facilitate the transformation, formation or deformation of aggregates in response to pH changes, making them widely used in the design and preparation of pH-responsive materials. Consequently, DMAEMA was selected in this study to prepare pH-responsive carbon-dot-based emulsifiers. Specifically, pH-responsive PDMAEMA-grafted CDs were synthesized through free radical polymerization, utilizing a crosslinking agent (methacrylic anhydride) and an initiator (potassium persulfate) [36]. The amino and carboxyl functional groups present in the carbon-dot based polymers facilitate the creation of pH responsive emulsions [37–39].

Regarding the responsiveness of carbon dots, the more common types are metal ion response [40], light stimulation [41], mechanical response [42], and pH response [9,10]. The pH response reported here is different from the conventional response, i.e., the change in pH is used to change the hydrophilicity of carbon dots for emulsification/breaking applications. In the study, two distinct types of carbon dots served as substrates for the grafting of DMAEMA monomers via free radical polymerization, resulting in the preparation of two pH-responsive Pickering emulsions. The experimental findings revealed while both carbon-dot-based emulsifiers exhibited stable emulsification across a broad range of oil phases, they demonstrated opposing responses to aqueous phase pH stimulation. To elucidate this phenomenon, an in-depth analysis of the pH responsiveness of these two carbon-point-based emulsifiers was conducted, introducing the concept of HLB value from a novel perspective.

The chemical reagents utilized in this study included: citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5 %), toluene, ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, 99 %), methacrylic anhydride (C8H10O3, 94 %, containing 0.2 % topanol stabiliser), potassium persulphate (K2S2O8, 99 %), and dimethylammonioethyl methacrylate (C8H15NO2, 99 %, containing 1000 ppm MEHQ stabiliser), all which were purchased from Aladdin Company, Shanghai, China. (C8H15NO2, 99 %, containing 1000 ppm MEHQ stabiliser) were purchased from Aladdin Company, Shanghai, China. Additionally, urea (H2NCONH2, 99.5 %), triethylamine (C6H15N, 99.5 %), tetrahydrofuran, trichloromethane, liquid paraffin (99 %), ethanol (99.8 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36%–38 %), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR) were sourced from Shanghai Sinopharm Holding Co. Deionized water was used throughout all experiments. All chemical reagents were employed without any further purification or treatment.

Citric acid (1 g) and urea (2 g) were thoroughly mixed in deionized water (20 ml). The resulting mixture was then heated in a 750 W microwave oven for 15 min until to a black solid was obtain. Subsequently, the black solid was dispersed in deionized water to ensure complete dissolution, followed by centrifugation. The supernatant was collected and further purified by dialysis using a 3500 MWCO dialysis bag for 48 h. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain the microwave-synthesized carbon dots, designated as M-CD-1. The quantum yield of the microwave-synthesized carbon dots obtained was approximately 32 %.

Citric acid (3.15 g) and ethylenediamine (1 ml) were thoroughly dissolved in deionized water (30 ml). The resulting solution was then transferred into a PTFE-lined reactor and heated at 200 °C for 5 h to obtain a brown solution. Following the reaction, the brown solution was centrifuged to remove any impurities. The supernatant was collected and subsequently dialyzed using a 3500 MWCO dialysis bag for 48 h. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain the desired hydrothermal carbon dots, labeled as H-CD-1. The quantum yield of the synthesized hydrothermal carbon dots was approximately 45 %.

The carbon dots (0.43 g), previously obtained from the previous steps, were dispersed in deionized water (30 ml). To this dispersion, triethylamine (0.6 g) and methacrylic anhydride (Met) (2.6 ml) were added, and the mixture was stirred thoroughly. The resulting solution was then heated at 55 °C for 24 h to allow for the grafting reaction to occur. Following this, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain solid particles. These solid particles were subsequently washed thoroughly with CHCl3 to remove any unreacted reagents or byproducts. Finally, the washed particles were dried in an oven to obtain the methacrylic anhydride grafted carbon dots, denoted as CD–NH–Met NPs.

The CD–NH–Met NPs (0.4 g), previously prepared in Section 2.4, were dispersed in deionized water (30 ml). To this dispersion, dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate (1.5 ml) and potassium persulfate (0.02 g) as an initiator were added. The mixture was then stirred for 48 h at 70 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere to ensure the controlled polymerization of DMAEMA onto the surface of the CD–NH–Met NPs. After the completion of the reaction, the resulting solution was freeze-dried to obtain solid particles. This solid was subsequently washed with tetrahydrofuran to remove any unreacted monomers or oligomers. Finally, the washed particles was dried in an oven to obtain poly [2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate] grafted carbon dots, denoted as CD–NH–Met-PDMAEMA NPs.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was employed to characterize the morphology, particle size and particle dispersion of the carbon dots. An X-ray Diffractometer (XRD) and a fluorescence spectrometer were utilized to confirm the successful synthesis of the carbon dots. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker Optics, Germany) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) are used to analyze the functional groups and elemental species present in the nanoparticles, providing valuable insight into their molecular structure. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) was applied to analyze the structural changes of the nanoparticles before and after polymer grafting, and a Zeta Potential Analyzer was used to measure the surface charge of the nanoparticles, which is crucial for interpreting the micromechanisms involved in their stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emulsification and demulsification of pickering emulsions
ce:section.ce:para: The Pickering emulsion was prepared using paraffin oil and deionized water as the incompatible oil and water phases, respectively. A 0.3 wt% Pickering emulsion was prepared by 21 mg of CD-3 nanoparticles (prepared in Section 2.5) were dispersed in 7 ml of deionized water. Subsequently, 7 ml of paraffin oil was added, and the resulting mixture was homogenized at 8000 r/min for 2 min to achieve a stable emulsion. The stability of the emulsion was evaluated through both visual inspection and measurement of the hydrodynamic diameter. Additionally, the influence of various parameters such as pH change, oil-to-water ratio and nanoparticle emulsifier concentration on the stability of the emulsions were systematically investigated. To test the pH responsive behavior of the emulsions, the pH of the emulsions was adjusted using 0.5 mol/L aqueous solutions of HCl and NaOH. This adjustment allowed for the examination of how changes in the pH environment affected the stability and potential demulsification of the Pickering emulsions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CD NPs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, two pH-responsive carbon-dot-based Pickering emulsifiers, designated as H-CD-1 and M-CD-1, were successfully prepared through via distinct methods. As shown in Fig. 1 , H-CD-1 was synthesized through a hydrothermal process using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors, whereas M-CD-1 was synthesized via a microwave method employing citric acid and urea. To create the methacrylic anhydride-modified carbon dots (CD-2), CD-1 was reacted with methacrylic anhydride in the presence of triethylamine and subjected to heating for 24 h. Subsequently, poly [2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate] was grafted onto the surface of CD-2 through a free radical polymerization process, catalyzed by potassium persulfate under an inert nitrogen atmosphere, resulting in the final product, CD-3.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, presented in Fig. 2 a and b, reveal the uniform distribution of both types of carbon dots (M-CD-1 and H-CD-1). Specifically, M-CD-1 exhibits an average particle size of 9.03 nm, with a size distribution mainly ranging from 6 to 15 nm. Similarly, H-CD-1 displays an average particle size of 6.21 nm, with a distribution mostly between 2 and 10 nm. In Fig. 2c, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of M-CD-1 exhibits three distinct absorption bands at 249, 330 and 418 nm. The first band is attributed to the π-π∗ absorption of CC core, while the latter two are attributed to the n-π∗ transition of the CO and CN bonds, respectively. Notably, the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths of M-CD-1 are 408 and 502 nm, respectively. In contrast, the absorption spectrum of H-CD-1, shown in Fig. 2d, displays peaks at 224 and 342 nm, associated with π-electron transition and the n-π∗ transition of the CO bond, respectively. The maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths for H-CD-1 are 257 and 650 nm, respectively. These unique fluorescence properties confirm the successful synthesis of both carbon dots. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the two carbon dots, presented in Fig. 2e, exhibit distinct peaks around 27.2° and 21°, respectively, indicative of a typical disordered graphite structure, consistent with previously reported spectra of CDs [43].

Fig. 3 a presents the Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra of the three M-CDs. Compared to M-CD-1, notable new characteristic absorption bands emerge in the spectra of M-CD-2 and M-CD-3. Specifically, a peak at 1462 cm−1, assigned to –CH2 stretching vibrations, emerges in the spectra of M-CD-2 and M-CD-3. Additionally, there is a sequential increase in the intensity of absorption peaks centered at 1080 and 938 cm−1, corresponding to the C–O and C–N bonds, respectively, found in methacrylic anhydride and DMAEMA. This progressive enhancement indicates the successful incorporation of these functional groups into the M-CDs. Similarly, Fig. 3b presents the FTIR spectra of H-CD, revealing a progressive increase in intensity of the absorption peaks at 1160 and 934 cm−1, mirroring the trend observed in the M-CD series. This consistency indicates successful grafting of polymer brushes onto both M-CD and H-CD samples. To further substantiate the successful grafting of polymer brushes, Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) hydrogen spectra of M-CD-3 and H-CD-3 are provided in Fig. 3c. A comparison of the spectral data reveals that both nanoparticles exhibit largely overlapping peaks, corresponding to the hydrogen atoms present within the polymer brushes. This similarity in spectral signatures underscores the successful grafting and formation of polymer brushes on both M-CD-3 and H-CD-3.

Fig. 4 demonstrates a comparison of the XPS spectra before and after the modification of polymers with carbon dots. Regarding the hydrothermal carbon dots (H-CDs), the comparison of Fig. 4(a), (d) with (c), (f) reveals that the area of the C–O characteristic peaks is significantly larger after modification. Additionally, these characteristic peaks shifted towards higher binding energy, with the peaks in the C1s scanning spectra shifting from 285.2 to 286 eV. This shift is primarily attributed to the formation of new double bonds between a large number of carbon atoms in the C–O groups and other oxygen atoms. The results in a relative decrease in the electron cloud density around the carbon atom, which subsequently affects the position of the characteristic peaks. In Fig. 4(b) and (e), we compare the XPS spectra of polymers before and after modification of carbon dots. The main reason for the observed shifts is the same as mentioned above: the formation of new double bonds between carbon atoms in C–O groups and other oxygen atoms leads to a decrease in electron cloud density on the carbon atoms. Additionally, the increased characteristic peak area of C–N in Fig. 4(b) and (e) indicates that the hydrothermal carbon dots are successfully grafted onto the polymers. Regarding the microwave carbon dots (M-CDs), Fig. 4i and (l) show an increase in the O–C characteristic peaks. Furthermore, the comparison of Fig. 4h and (k) reveals a new characteristic peak for the amide bond in the M-CD-3 spectrum, located at 402.2 eV. This directly indicates that PDMAEMA was successfully modified on the surface of the microwave carbon dots.

The impact of distinct carbon dot structures on oil recovery were investigated by comparing the effects of injecting two carbon-dot-based emulsifiers, H-CD-3 and M-CD-3, each at a concentration of 500 ppm, into a micro-model characterized a heterogeneous void structure. The primary objective was to evaluate their influence on the recovery of heavy crude oil. Additionally, the movement behavior of brine and nanofluids containing carbon dots within the micro-modelled porous media during oil displacement was investigated and visually recorded through continuous microscopic imaging. This methodology aimed to elucidate the fundamental mechanism by which carbon dots enhance oil recovery. The results showed that the nanofluid incorporating carbon dots significantly enhanced the oil displacement efficiency compared to the sole injection of brine. This enhancement is primarily attributed to the fact that, upon brine injection, the wall of the micro-model remain oleophobic, restricting brine flow primarily to the center of the pores, unable to effectively displace oil adhered to the wall. By analysing the photographs of pure brine repulsion in Fig. 5 b, the displacement factor of brine was obtained to be 42.3 ± 0.9 %. In contrast, when the nanofluid containing carbon dots permeates the system, CD-3 adsorbs onto the pore walls of the micro-model, altering their wettability. This transformation enables oil droplets originally attached to the wall to detach, allowing the nanofluid to flow not only through the center of the pore but also along the wall surface, thereby dramatically improving the crude oil displacement efficiency. Fig. 5c and d shows the record images of the microscopic model at the end of waterflooding with brine containing 500 ppm concentration of H-CD-3 and M-CD-3, respectively, in order to study the effect of nanofluid flow on the oil displacement. Under H-CD-3 and M-CD-3 conditions, the displacement factor were 81.8 ± 2.1 % and 86.2 ± 1.8 %, respectively. These findings emphasize the crucial role of carbon dots in modifying porous media wettability as a key mechanism for enhanced oil recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CD-3-stabilized pickering emulsion
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the influence of varying nanoparticle concentrations and oil-to-water ratios on emulsions stability, a series of Pickering emulsions were formulated in this study, each containing nanoparticles at incrementally concentrations and distinct oil-to-water ratios. Through experimental trials, the optimal nanoparticle concentration and oil-to-water ratio were identified, establishing a consistent baseline for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of oil-to-water ratio on emulsion stability
ce:section.ce:para: This section examines the influence of oil-to-water ratio on the stability of Pickering emulsions. By adjusting the paraffin-to-water from 1:3 to 3:1, and homogenizing at 8000 r/min for 2 min, a series of emulsion was obtained. As shown in Fig. 6 a, for emulsions stabilized with H-CD-3, when the oil volume exceeds that of water, the emulsion undergoes rapid destabilization. In contrast, when the oil-to-water ratio shifts from 1:3 to 1:1, the emulsions exhibit improved dispersibility and a more uniform droplet size. Balancing emulsification efficiency and cost-effectiveness, an oil-to-water ratio of 1:1 was deemed optimal. Fig. 6b presents the behavior of emulsions stabilized with M-CD-3. Notably, at an oil-to-water ratio of 3:1, the emulsion was highly unstable. When the ratio was reduced to 2:1, the emulsion droplet size was obviously larger than the other three emulsions, compromising its stability. For M-CD-3, decreasing the proportion of the oil phase results in smaller emulsion droplets. Conversely, an excess of oil phase leads to larger droplets and relatively poor stability. This phenomenon can be attributed to the fact that, a higher concentration of one phase leads to a lower density of nanoparticles at the droplet interface, raising the interfacial tension between oil and water. This increased tension promotes the coalescence of smaller droplets, ultimately weakening the emulsion's stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nanoparticle concentration on emulsion stability
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of Pickering emulsions is closely related to both the size distribution of the emulsion droplets and the concentration of NPs employed. Fig. 7 demonstrates the influence of varying NP concentrations on the emulsion morphology. It was observed that emulsions stabilized with nanoparticle concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 0.5 wt% remained stable, showing no notable oil-water separation even after one week of storage. The influence of these two emulsifier concentrations on the emulsion droplet size was fundamentally similar, with an increase in nanoparticle concentration leading to a decrease in droplet size. Notably, after the concentration surpassed 0.3 wt%, further increments led to diminishingly smaller changes in droplet size, suggesting that the emulsion stability enhances with rising nanoparticle concentration and eventually reaches a plateau. This phenomenon can be primarily attributed to the fact that, at lower nanoparticle concentrations, the coverage at the oil-water interface is insufficient to form a dense protective layer, allowing small droplets to coalesce under interfacial tension forces, thereby compromising emulsion stability. Conversely, at a concentration of 0.5 % wt%, the emulsion becomes highly viscous and adheres strongly to the container walls. This is a result of the formation of a reticulated network by the high concentration of nanoparticles in the continuous phase, which hinders the fluidity of the entire system.

The emulsion system containing 0.3 wt% CD-3 and an equal volume ratio of the two phases exhibited excellent stability, with no observable alteration in the emulsion droplets after over a period of seven days. Additionally, the paraffin-in-water emulsion system was verified through a drop test, where a droplet of the emulsion was introduced into deionized water to observe its dispersion behavior. In summary, CD-3 emerges as an efficient and stable emulsifier for the system studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of salt ion concentration on emulsion stability
ce:section.ce:para: The salt resistance of the two emulsifiers was analyzed and investigated. NaCl was chosen to formulate aqueous solutions of salt ions with different concentration gradients, which served as the aqueous phase of the emulsions. An equal volume of paraffin oil was added to formulate the emulsions. When the concentration of Na+ was increased from 0 mol/L to 4 mol/L, the size of the emulsion droplets primarily remained within the range of 20–280 μm. When the salt ion concentration exceeded 4 mol/L (corresponding to a water mineralisation of 2.7 × 105 mg/L), extremely stable emulsions could not be formed. Both emulsifiers exhibited similar behavior in a salt-ion environment: the average droplet size gradually increased with rising salt-ion concentration (Fig. 8 a–d, and Fig. 8f–i). However, the magnitude of the increase was not significant, with a change in average size of no more than 8 % for each increase in the concentration gradient. Concurrently, with the increase of salt ion concentration, the water separation rate also increased (as shown in Fig. 8e and j). Notably, the most significant increase in water separation rate occurred when the salt ion concentration rose from 0 to 1 mol/L. These findings indicate that the presence of salt ions does have a certain effect on the morphology and stability of the emulsion. Nevertheless, overall, the emulsifiers demonstrated a certain level of tolerance to salt ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-responsive behavior of CD-3-stabilized pickering emulsion
ce:section.ce:para: Both carbon dot-based emulsifiers investigated in this study exhibited pH-responsive behavior, albeit with notable differences in their responsiveness. The Pickering emulsions prepared in this section were obtained by mixing and homogenizing 0.3 wt% CD-3, 7 ml of water, 7 ml paraffin oil. The pH of the emulsions was adjusted using 0.5 mol/L solution of HCl and NaOH.

As shown in Fig. 9 a, H-CD-3 emulsified effectively under alkaline conditions (pH = 10), and the addition of acid to the system led to demulsification (pH = 5). In contrast, M-CD-3 emulsified under acidic conditions (pH = 5) and demulsified under alkaline conditions (pH = 9). Fig. 9b specifically illustrates that, after emulsification, the two phases separated, with the majority of H-CD-3 nanoparticles residing in the aqueous phase, causing it to turn light yellow. This phenomenon can be attributed to the high degree of protonation of the terminal dimethylamino groups on PDMAEMA chains attached to the CD NPs under acidic conditions [44]. Since the carbon dots themselves are hydrophilic, the hydrophobicity of the attached organic long-chain polymers diminishes further under acidic conditions, resulting in an overall increase in hydrophilicity that prevents stable emulsification at the oil-water interface. With the addition of alkali, the dimethylamino groups become deprotonated, enabling the formation of a stable emulsion once again. Notably, this emulsification-demulsification cycle can be repeated seven times in the H-CD-3 system without significant loss of reversibility.

In the case of M-CD-3, emulsification occurs under acidic conditions, with demulsification observed under alkaline conditions. The emulsification-demulsification cycle can be repeated five times without significant changes in emulsion stability. For both emulsifiers, the presence of salt ions in the environment had no notable effect on their responsiveness. As the salt ion concentration accumulated with each responsive emulsion-breaking cycle, the responsiveness of the emulsifiers did remained unaffected. Fig. 9c shows the state of the emulsion after seven and five cycles of responsiveness for H-CD-3 and M-CD-3, respectively, and there is indeed a tendency for the droplet size to increase. Although the dimethylamino group on PDMAEMA remain protonated under acidic conditions, and the matrix carbon dots are inherently hydrophilic, the response behavior of M-CD-3 differs significantly from that of H-CD-3. This discrepancy is explained by considering the combined effects of the emulsifiers' hydrophilic-lipophilic balance (HLB) and protonation state. The unique interaction between these factors results in the distinct pH-responsive behaviors observed for H-CD-3 and M-CD-3.

PDMAEMA contains tertiary amine groups that exhibit weak electrolyte properties, and its protonation process is significantly influenced by the pH of the solution. Under acidic conditions, the tertiary amine group readily undergo protonated, whereas in an alkaline environment, they deprotonate from their protonated state back to the neutral tertiary amine form. This protonation-deprotonation process can be quantitatively determined by measuring the Zeta potential of the nanoparticles at various pH conditions. By adjusting the system's pH through the dropwise addition of NaOH and HCl, it is possible to controllably switch between emulsification and demulsification in the emulsion system. Experiments were conducted to analyze the pH and Zeta potential of two modified carbon dots suspensions, H-CD-3 and M-CD-3, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 10 , the zeta potential of H-CD-3 fluctuates between −33.2 and −18.3 mV when the pH changes between 3 and 13. Similarly, for M-CD-3, the pH change interval is also from 3 to 13, and its zeta potential fluctuates from −35.3 to −18.3 mV. It is clearly seen that the zeta potentials of the above two carbons decrease with the increase of pH, but their pH response ranges are not particularly wide. For H-CD-3, the effective pH response range is approximately 5–10; for M-CD-3, the effective response range is about 5–9. Excessively acidic or alkaline environments cause the amide bonds in the emulsifiers to break, leading to a loss of responsiveness. The subsequent change in nanoparticle zeta potential after bond breakage is attributed to the appearance of the primary amines group following hydrolysis. These findings closely correlate with the protonation and deprotonation processes of the amine groups, suggesting that the emulsification and demulsification transitions observed in both modified carbon dot emulsifiers originate from the reversible protonation of their surface functional groups. Thus, the emulsification and demulsification transitions of these two carbon-dot emulsifiers are closely related to the pH-responsive nature of their tertiary amine groups.

Currently, most of the response mechanisms of pH-responsive emulsifiers involve protonation and deprotonation. For instance, Dong's team synthesized MOF-based emulsifiers with pH-responsive emulsifiers [26]. During the response process, the amino group on the polymer chain undergoes protonation first, allowing the hydrophilic polymer chain to achieve a hydrophilic-oleophilic equilibrium with the hydrophobic MOF. The equilibrium stabilized the interface between oil and water. Subsequently, deprotonation occurs, making the polymer chain more hydrophobic again, at which point the emulsifier returned to the oil phase. This represents a significant breakthrough in emulsification. Additionally, pH-responsive emulsification can occur through the breaking and formation of dynamic imine bonds. For example, Sun et al. [45] connected hydrophilic silica nanoparticles with hydrophobic benzaldehyde through dynamic imine bonding using the Schiff base reaction. Under acidic conditions, the imine bond breaks, causing the two parts to desorb from the oil-water interface and enabling rapid separation of oil and water. Under alkaline conditions, re-bonding occurs, leading to the formation of stable Pickering emulsions again.

In this study, the synthesized substances do not contain dynamic imine bonds but do possess a significant number of tertiary amino groups capable of protonation and deprotonation. Therefore, the response mechanism of both emulsifiers will undoubtedly include protonation and deprotonation processes. To elucidate the distinct responsiveness exhibited by the two carbon-dot-based emulsifiers, this study initiated an analysis centered on the hydrophilic-lipophilic balance (HLB) value, aiming to approximate the HLB values of the two emulsifiers. Following Griffin's principle [46], stable emulsions can be formulated using surfactants or surfactant blends that match the HLB value of the oil phase match. This principle guides the choice of appropriate surfactants or even surfactant combinations for the production of stable emulsion systems [47]. To determine the approximate HLB values of our emulsifiers, a series of oil samples with varying HLB values were prepared, using toluene (HLB 15) and paraffin (HLB 4) as reference points. Each emulsifiers was used to emulsify these oil samples under identical conditions, and the stability of the resulting emulsions was observed. The HLB value of the oil sample yielded the most stable emulsion was considered to be closest to the HLB value of the emulsifier [48,49]. The HLB value of the mixed oil phase was calculated using the following formula, where xi represents the mass fraction of each oil component:
H L B
t o t a l
= ∑
H L B
i
∗
x i

To further investigate the performance disparities between the two carbon-dot based emulsifiers, an initial comparison was undertaken focusing on the macroscopic and microscopic morphologies of the emulsions they produced. However, as shown in Table 1 , there were no notable differences in these macroscopic and microscopic morphologies between the emulsions from the two sets, making it challenging to discern which emulsifier emulsified the oil samples more effectively based solely on these observations. Subsequently, the stability of the emulsions was evaluated through the analysis of their water partition rate. Fig. 11 illustrates that the two emulsifiers possess distinct optimal HLB values: M-CD-3 emulsifies oils most effectively at an HLB value of approximately 8, whereas H-CD-3 performs optimally at an HLB value of around 15. According to Griffin's research, oil-in-water emulsifiers typically exhibit HLB values within the range of 8–17 [46]. Notably, these two emulsifiers occupy the extremes of this HLB range. The disparate behaviors of these emulsifiers can be explained by their varying protonation levels across different pH environments. For M-CD-3, under conditions where a large number of its tertiary amino groups are protonated, the overall hydrophilicity increases significantly, causing the HLB value of M-CD-3 shift into the optimal range for water-in-oil emulsifiers, thereby enabling stable emulsion formation. However, in an alkaline environment, it becomes deprotonated, and the nanoparticles become more lipophilic, resulting in the HLB value of M-CD-3 falling below the optimal range for such emulsifiers, leading to emulsion destabilization. Conversely, when H-CD-3 is deprotonated with a significant amount of tertiary amino groups, its lipophilicity increases significantly, and its HLB value decreases to within the range of the ideal HLB value for water-in-oil emulsifiers, which facilitates the formation of emulsions. However, upon protonation, its hydrophilicity increases, driving its HLB value beyond the optimal range and triggering emulsion destabilization or breakage.

It is noteworthy that despite the subsequent synthesis procedures and the addition of various chemicals being identical, with the sole exception of the synthetic method (microwave versus hydrothermal), the two emulsifiers exhibited contrasting pH responses. Furthermore, this notable disparity in their HLB (Hydrophile-Lipophile Balance) values can be attributed to the distinct protonation processes they undergo in response to pH variations.

In summary, this study successfully developed a straightforward approach to fabricating smart pH-responsive oil-in-water Pickering emulsion systems utilizing two distinct types of carbon dots. It further provides a mechanistic explanation for the opposing pH response behaviors exhibited by the two emulsifiers. By adopting widely practiced synthesis routes, namely microwave and hydrothermal methods, followed by uniform modification strategies, we synthesized these carbon dots. Subsequently, the intermediates were modified with methacrylic anhydride and finally grafted with poly [2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate] (PDMAEMA). Our experimental findings reveal that both carbon dot-based emulsifiers (designated as CD-3) were capable of stabilizing paraffin-water Pickering emulsions under natural conditions, yet they exhibit contrasting pH response behavior. The study offers a profound mechanistic understanding of these smart pH-induced emulsification and demulsification phenomena, which are attributed to the reversible protonation and deprotonation of amino groups on PDMAEMA, thereby modulating the hydrophilic-lipophilic balance (HLB) values. Despite these achievements, the work is not without limitations. Specifically, the yield of carbon dots is not very high, leading to relatively high costs. Additionally, carbon dots are still less utilized in the field of responsive emulsification, and the overall technology is not yet mature enough. However, these limitations do not detract from the significance of this research. Overall, this research presents a simple and practical strategy for the intelligent manipulation of carbon dot-based emulsifiers in emulsification/demulsification processes, with promising applications in areas such as drug transport, oil recovery, fluorescence-triggered separation, and wastewater treatment.

Tianhao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Junjie Fan: Investigation. Wenxiu Qiu: Investigation. Xinyu Yuan: Formal analysis. Jianwen Hu: Data curation. Haotian Gao: Conceptualization. Wenqing Xie: Data curation. Chunling Li: Data curation. Shuangqing Sun: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Songqing Hu: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
